title: How to Use a Body Scan for Anxiety Relief
excerpt: Learn how to use a body scan to calm anxiety by tuning into your body's signals and rebalancing your nervous system effectively.
body:
Understanding Anxiety Through the Body

Anxiety isn’t just a mental phenomenon—it’s a whole-body experience. When anxiety strikes, your nervous system reacts with tension, racing heartbeats, shallow breathing, and sometimes a flood of overwhelming sensations. This is your body’s survival response kicking in, driven by the autonomic nervous system, which controls involuntary reactions to threat.

Pioneering trauma expert Bessel van der Kolk writes extensively about how trauma and anxiety are stored in the body, creating patterns of somatic dysregulation that persist even when the original threat is gone. Our bodies remember, and without tools to regulate these responses, the anxiety can feel inescapable.

So how do you break this cycle? The answer lies in tuning into your body, not fighting it. That’s where the body scan comes in.

What Is a Body Scan?

A body scan is a mindfulness-based somatic practice that involves systematically paying attention to different parts of the body, noticing sensations without judgment. It’s not about trying to change what you feel but simply observing and allowing yourself to be present with your internal experience.

Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, emphasizes that somatic awareness is key to re-regulating the nervous system and healing trauma. When you become aware of bodily sensations linked to anxiety, you activate your prefrontal cortex, the brain’s rational center. This helps you regain a sense of control and calm.

The body scan, often used in mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR), helps shift your nervous system from the sympathetic “fight or flight” mode to the parasympathetic “rest and digest” state. This shift is crucial for anxiety relief.

Why a Body Scan Works for Anxiety Relief

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how the vagus nerve mediates your body’s response to stress and safety. When anxious, your nervous system is often stuck in a defensive state. The body scan encourages awareness and safety signals that can help engage the ventral vagal complex—your nervous system’s “safe and social” branch.

By scanning your body, you:

  • Identify where anxiety manifests physically (e.g., chest tightness, stomach knots)
  • Allow your nervous system to recognize these sensations as safe to feel
  • Interrupt automatic stress responses by directing attention inward
  • Promote grounding and present-moment awareness

Research shows that body-focused mindfulness techniques reduce anxiety symptoms by decreasing amygdala activation, the brain’s fear center, and improving emotional regulation (Francine Shapiro, known for EMDR, also highlights the importance of body awareness in trauma processing).

How to Do a Body Scan for Anxiety Relief

Here is a step-by-step guide to doing a body scan. Find a quiet space where you won't be disturbed. You can do this lying down, sitting, or even standing if necessary.

Step 1: Settle and Breathe

Close your eyes if that feels comfortable. Take a few slow, deep breaths. Inhale through your nose for about 4 seconds, hold for 2 seconds, then exhale gently through your mouth for 6 seconds. This breath cycle helps activate your parasympathetic nervous system (Deb Dana’s work on polyvagal-informed breath regulation is a great resource).

Step 2: Bring Gentle Attention to Your Feet

Focus on your toes and feet. Notice any sensations: warmth, coolness, tingling, pressure against the floor or surface. Don’t try to change anything. Simply observe.

Step 3: Slowly Move Up the Body

Move your attention gradually to your ankles, calves, knees, thighs, pelvis, lower back, abdomen, chest, shoulders, arms, hands, neck, and finally your head and face. Spend about 20-30 seconds on each area.

Step 4: Notice Sensations Without Judgment

If you feel tension, pain, or discomfort, acknowledge it silently: “There is tightness here,” or “I notice warmth.” If you don’t feel anything, that’s okay too.

Step 5: Stay Present and Breathe

Keep your breathing slow and steady. If your mind wanders, gently guide it back to the part of the body you’re scanning.

Step 6: Finish with Full-Body Awareness

After scanning all body parts, bring your awareness to your body as a whole. Notice how it feels to be present inside your body in this moment.

What to Expect When You Start Body Scanning

Newcomers to body scans might feel discomfort or restlessness. This is normal—your nervous system isn’t used to this kind of focused internal attention. Anxiety might initially rise as you become aware of sensations you've been avoiding.

Gabor Maté stresses that healing trauma means allowing these sensations to be felt safely, not suppressing or avoiding them. Over time, with consistent practice, the body scan can help desensitize these sensations and reduce anxiety overall.

“The body scan is a doorway into the present moment where anxiety loses its grip, and your nervous system can begin to settle.” — The Oracle Lover

Tips for Maximizing the Benefits of Body Scans

  • Practice regularly: Daily or several times a week is ideal.
  • Use guided recordings: Audio body scans by teachers like Jon Kabat-Zinn or Tara Brach can help you stay focused.
  • Be patient: Healing takes time. Don’t expect instant relief.
  • Combine with other somatic tools: Techniques like grounding, movement, or breathwork can deepen regulation.
  • Adapt the scan: If long scans feel overwhelming, start with shorter sessions focusing on just a few body parts.

When to Seek Professional Support

While body scans are powerful, they aren't a substitute for professional help when anxiety is severe or linked to trauma. If you notice that body scanning triggers intense distress or flashbacks, it’s important to reach out to trauma-informed therapists experienced in somatic approaches like Sensorimotor Psychotherapy (Pat Ogden), EMDR (Francine Shapiro), or Internal Family Systems (Richard Schwartz).

Somatic work is best done with professional guidance when your nervous system feels overwhelmed or stuck in hyperarousal or dissociation.

Summary: Body Scans as a Pathway to Anxiety Relief

Body scans offer a scientifically grounded, accessible way to engage your nervous system in healing anxiety. By cultivating mindful awareness of your body’s signals, you create the conditions for nervous system regulation and emotional resilience. This practice respects the wisdom of your body, allowing you to move from surviving anxiety to living with greater ease.

Start slow, stay consistent, and remember that your body is your ally—not your enemy—in the journey toward relief.

faqs.q: How long should a body scan for anxiety relief take?
faqs.a: Body scans can last anywhere from 5 to 30 minutes. Beginners might start with shorter sessions and gradually increase as they become more comfortable.

faqs.q: Can body scans replace therapy for anxiety?
faqs.a: Body scans are a helpful self-directed tool but aren’t a substitute for therapy, especially for severe anxiety or trauma. They work best as a complement to professional support.

faqs.q: What if I feel more anxious during the body scan?
faqs.a: It’s common to notice increased anxiety initially. The key is to stay gentle and curious with your sensations. If distress becomes intense, pause and seek guidance from a qualified therapist.

faqs.q: How often should I practice body scans to see results?
faqs.a: Consistency matters. Practicing body scans several times a week or daily can help retrain your nervous system and reduce anxiety over time.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma and anxiety are stored in the body and the importance of somatic healing.

metaTitle: Body Scan for Anxiety Relief: How to Use It Effectively
